1: The obligation to testify publicly when called — the one who hears the public adjuration and withholds testimony bears guilt. Christ perfectly fulfills the opposite: he testifies truthfully before Pilate about his own identity (John 18:37: 'For this I was born and for this I have come into the world — to bear witness to the truth') even knowing the testimony will lead to his death. Where Israel failed to testify, the one faithful Witness (Revelation 1:5: ho martus ho pistos) bears witness without flinching.
2: Inadvertent contact with uncleanness creates guilt even without awareness. Christ enters the full range of human uncleanness not inadvertently but deliberately: he touches the leper (Mark 1:41), the corpse (Luke 7:14), the hemorrhaging woman (Mark 5:27-29) — reversing the transmission direction. Where Levitical contact made the clean unclean, his contact makes the unclean clean. He bears the inadvertent-contamination category by experiencing it in full and overcoming it.
3: Human uncleanness contracted unknowingly still creates liability. The mechanism of Christ's atonement covers not only known sins but the full range of human guilt, including what was not known to be guilt at the time: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34). The intercessory prayer at the cross addresses precisely this inadvertent category of sin — the crucifiers do not know they are sinning against the Son of God.
4: The rash oath forgotten — impulsive speech that becomes a forgotten liability. Christ's atonement covers the full range of speech sins, including the careless words that humans dismiss as insignificant: 'I tell you, on the day of judgment people will give account for every careless word they speak' (Matthew 12:36). The rash-oath category confirms that the atonement system takes seriously what human consciousness often minimizes.
5: Confession (viduy) must precede the offering: 'he shall confess the sin he has committed.' The verbal acknowledgment is not merely procedural but constitutive of the atonement transaction. This structure is the type of 1 John 1:9: 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.' The pattern is identical: verbal acknowledgment → priestly atonement → forgiveness. Christ's sacrifice provides the permanent atoning mechanism; the confession is the point of application.
6: The female animal as the base-level sin offering for the graduated scale — accessible to ordinary Israelites. The chatat system's design goal is universal atonement access; no Israelite is too poor for atonement. Christ's atonement is offered without cost to the spiritually poor: 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come, buy and eat' (Isaiah 55:1). The graduated chatat's sliding scale is the type of atonement offered equally to rich and poor, 'without money and without price.'
7: The first tier of poverty provision: two birds when a lamb is unaffordable. The system refuses to price the poor out of atonement. Christ is born into precisely this economic category: Mary's offering at the purification (Luke 2:24: 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons') is the poor person's bird offering of Leviticus 12:8, confirming that the one who would provide final atonement for all entered the world in the economic position of those for whom the poverty provision was designed.
8: The bird sin offering — wringing the neck without severing completely. The violence of the sacrifice is inescapable even in the minimal form; the bird must die. Hebrews 9:22: 'without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.' The scaled-down offering maintains the irreducible requirement: blood must be shed. The smallest offering and the largest point to the same necessity that Christ's death fulfills.
9: The blood sprinkled on the altar side, remainder drained at the base. The partial blood manipulation of the bird offering accomplishes the same atoning function as the full blood manipulation of the lamb. The efficacy does not scale with the size of the offering; the blood of any legitimate sacrifice atones because YHWH has given blood for atonement (Leviticus 17:11). Christ's blood does not atone in proportion to his greatness but because the Father appointed it as the one final atonement.
10: The second bird as a burnt offering — complete consecration following atonement. The two-bird sequence (chatat + olah) rehearses the pattern that frames Levitical worship: first remove the barrier of sin (chatat), then offer the life fully to God (olah). Christ's death on the cross is both: the sin offering that removes the barrier and the burnt offering of total self-consecration to the Father ('Not my will, but yours be done,' Luke 22:42).
11: The flour offering for the absolutely poor — no oil, no frankincense, because it is for a sin offering. Even a handful of flour can accomplish atonement when brought with confession and priestly mediation. The minimal offering is still received as the maximum: Christ's single sacrifice receives the poorest confessing sinner exactly as it receives the one who comes with full understanding. Access is not proportional to resources.
12: The handful (kometz) burned as the memorial portion (azkarah) before YHWH. The handful represents the whole; the remainder becomes priestly food. Christ's single act of self-offering ('once for all,' Hebrews 7:27) represents and accomplishes atonement for all; the 'remainder' — the ongoing intercessory work — belongs to the eternal High Priest who 'always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25).
13: The identical atonement-forgiveness formula regardless of economic tier: 'the priest shall make atonement for him... and he shall be forgiven.' The flour offering produces the same forensic result as the lamb. The type teaches what the antitype accomplishes: Christ's one sacrifice atones equally for all regardless of the quality or quantity of the worshiper's approach. 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28).
14: The revelation formula introducing the guilt offering for sacrilege against holy things. The asham begins with the category of violations against YHWH's own portion. Christ's atonement addresses not only interpersonal sin but the sacrilege against God's holiness itself — the defilement of the sanctuary of creation by human sin that extends from the first Adam's transgression (Romans 5:12-21) to the accumulated desecration of God's world.
15: The guilt offering for unintentional sacrilege — failing to give YHWH what is rightly his. The asham addresses the chronic human failure to honor YHWH's proprietary claims on time, tithe, firstfruits, and devotion. Christ's active obedience, his perfect lifetime of giving YHWH everything that was due, is the positive fulfillment of the asham's requirement: not only removing the deficit through atonement but restoring what was owed in full through perfect covenant faithfulness.
16: The chomesh (one-fifth restitution surcharge): making good the violation plus twenty percent. Christ's active obedience exceeds mere substitutionary coverage of the debt; the surplus of his righteousness (perisseuein, the overflow of grace, Romans 5:17: 'those who receive the abundance of grace and the free gift of righteousness will reign in life through the one man Jesus Christ') is the antitype of the chomesh — Christ restores beyond the minimum, generating an overflow of righteousness that is credited to believers.
17: The asham for uncertain violations — the 'floating guilt' offering brought when one suspects but does not know a violation has occurred. Christ's atonement covers not only known sins but the full range of human failure including what was not perceived as sin. Paul: 'My conscience is clear, but that does not make me innocent' (1 Corinthians 4:4) — the category of unknown culpability that the precautionary asham addresses is permanently resolved by the comprehensive scope of Christ's sacrifice.
18: The ram of the guilt offering and the forgiveness formula: 'he shall be forgiven for the mistake that he made unintentionally.' Unintentional sin is no less covered by the asham than deliberate sin; the scope of forgiveness is total. Hebrews 10:10: 'by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all.' The completeness of Christ's offering covers the full spectrum of human culpability including the inadvertent.
19: The declarative formula: asham hu (it is a guilt offering) — the offering's identity is declared before it functions. The most important declaration in the NT is the parallel: Isaiah 53:10 uses the same term for the Servant: tasim asham nafesho (when you make his life a guilt offering / when his soul is made an asham). The Servant of YHWH's death is explicitly identified as an asham — not merely any sacrifice but the guilt-offering that covers sacrilege and requires the chomesh restitution. Christ's death is the fulfillment of the entire asham category.
